name: Ferdinand Blumentritt
role: Austrian teacher and ethnographer; Rizal's closest confidant

portrait.alt: Sepia studio portrait of Ferdinand Blumentritt, a bearded man wearing a pince-nez and dark coat

bio:
There is one friend whom Rizal shared over 200 letters with in their 10 years of friendship: Austrian Professor Ferdinand Blumentritt. Their friendship began when Rizal sent Blumentritt a copy of the Aritmética Redactada en Tagalo y Castellano, a book by Rufino Baltazar Hernandez intended to teach Filipinos arithmetic in the Tagalog language. The two had not yet met in person during this time.

Blumentritt was highly interested in the Philippines to the point that his home, filled with Filipino artifacts, objects, books and maps, looked like a Philippine museum. Rizal commended this fascination through Blumentritt's extensive study and analysis of Filipino culture and people, successfully giving a thorough and objective description of a country that he never visited. This scholarly pursuit is shown in his book Versuch einer Ethnographie der Philippinen (An Attempt at Writing a Philippine Ethnography), where he described the customs, traditions, and everyday life of the country's inhabitants.

Rizal and Blumentritt finally met at the professor's residence at Leitmeritz in May 1887, nearly a year after their first correspondence, through Maximo Viola. Blumentritt welcomed his guests like a hospitable Filipino, serving them adobo, lechon, lumpia, pancit, kare-kare, and paella. He also brought them to museums during their visit and even introduced them to leading citizens, making their meeting a minor state event published in the Leitmeritzer Zeitung (Leitmeritz Newspaper).

Distance did not hinder their friendship, as the two continued their correspondence with letters crossing seas and continents. Knowing Blumentritt's interest in the Philippines, Rizal sent him a sulpakan (fire piston), together with six cigarettes, sampaguitas, cinnamon, and kamuning flowers on 26 September 1887. Blumentritt wrote in his reply on 15 November 1887 that he was very much interested in the sulpakan, and that he would show it to physics professors as argumentum vivum (living evidence) of the great talent of the indios. In Rizal's donation of ethnographic objects to the Museum für Völkerkunde, he would also include a sulpakan similar to what he gifted Blumentritt.

Their letter exchanges continued during Rizal's exile in Dapitan. In a place that was unfamiliar to him, Rizal was consoled by his writing to Blumentritt, giving him a vivid account of his life in Mindanao: the houses he built, his everyday routine, the fields, the treatment of patients, and the time spent with his students.

Blumentritt commemorated his friend's life in his 1897 article José Rizal's Studies in Ethnic Psychology, citing his contributions to the transcendence of the Philippine Malay race in Europe. He wrote of him so highly: “Rizal was not only the most important man of his country, but also of the Malayan race as a whole. His memory will not fade in his own country, and future Spanish generations will yet speak the name of Rizal with respect.”

Before his execution, Rizal wrote one last letter to Blumentritt, a farewell not from a friend, but from a brother.
